clusters.title: The only way to stop petrol pump thieves | Brief letters
clusters.neutral_headline: The only way to stop petrol pump thieves | Brief letters

items.title: The only way to stop petrol pump thieves | Brief letters
items.standfirst: Fuelling up | Child health | Burnham spirit | DIY disasterI was once visiting Massachusetts in the US and forgot to take my card into the gas station before filling up (‘A guy did a runner in a Jaguar’: the petrol theft scandal – and the garage owner struggling to stop it, 21 July). After some loudspeaker remonstration, I took my card in, apologising that I’d forgotten as we don’t do that in the UK. The owner almost fell on the floor laughing. “How do you stop people stealing gas?” he asked. “We don’t,” I replied. Further merriment ensued. Sadly, it’s time we did start not releasing pumps until we have cash or a card deposit on hand.Paul TaroniAlton, Hampshire• I shall always remember the health visitor who came to see me after my youngest son was born, nearly 50 years ago (Letters, 21 July). She had the brilliant idea of asking me if I could deliver a message for her to another mother with a new baby living further up our north London street. I did, and we became firm friends – as did our sons. As your letter writers suggest, she was indeed addressing the wider social determinants of health. Bring them back!Bridget PattersonWoodbridge, Suffolk Continue reading...
items.content: Fuelling up | Child health | Burnham spirit | DIY disasterI was once visiting Massachusetts in the US and forgot to take my card into the gas station before filling up (‘A guy did a runner in a Jaguar’: the petrol theft scandal – and the garage owner struggling to stop it, 21 July). After some loudspeaker remonstration, I took my card in, apologising that I’d forgotten as we don’t do that in the UK. The owner almost fell on the floor laughing. “How do you stop people stealing gas?” he asked. “We don’t,” I replied. Further merriment ensued. Sadly, it’s time we did start not releasing pumps until we have cash or a card deposit on hand.Paul TaroniAlton, Hampshire• I shall always remember the health visitor who came to see me after my youngest son was born, nearly 50 years ago (Letters, 21 July). She had the brilliant idea of asking me if I could deliver a message for her to another mother with a new baby living further up our north London street. I did, and we became firm friends – as did our sons. As your letter writers suggest, she was indeed addressing the wider social determinants of health. Bring them back!Bridget PattersonWoodbridge, Suffolk Continue reading...

clusters.title: Poorest children in England 40% more likely to be hospitalised for lung infections
clusters.neutral_headline: Poorest children in England 40% more likely to be hospitalised for lung infections

items.title: Poorest children in England 40% more likely to be hospitalised for lung infections
items.standfirst: Exclusive: Analysis of NHS England data reveals stark impact of air pollution in deprived areasChildren living in the poorest areas across England are 40% more likely to be admitted to hospital for respiratory infections, an analysis of NHS England data shows, with experts raising the alarm on the impact of air pollution on young people’s health.The analysis, based on data obtained via freedom of information requests by the charity Impact on Urban Health, revealed 1,941 out of every 100,000 children living in the most deprived fifth of England were admitted to hospital for respiratory illnesses in 2024-25. Continue reading...

clusters.title: ‘Why do I feel crappy?’: women are learning about their luteal phases - and Maha is weaponizing it
clusters.neutral_headline: ‘Why do I feel crappy?’: women are learning about their luteal phases...
bullet_summary: “During the luteal phase, the reason why your stress threshold is so much lower is because this is the time your body would be incubating a potential baby,” she said

items.title: ‘Why do I feel crappy?’: women are learning about their luteal phases - and Maha is weaponizing it
items.standfirst: People have found language for moodiest part of their menstrual cycles, and the right has seized on itEmily Rae Detrick, a health coach, wants women to be more in touch with their menstrual cycle in its entirety. This, she said on an October episode of Culture Apothecary with Alex Clark, a podcast popular among “Make America Healthy Again” (Maha) adherents, is “a way that you honor God and the way we were created”. It also serves her self-assigned mission to help “make America fertile again”.In the episode and on social media, Detrick speaks at length about the luteal phase, the 14-ish days between ovulation and menstruation when women are more likely to experience irritability, bloating and food cravings. If a woman does not get pregnant after ovulating, or so Detrick’s line of thinking goes, her body becomes upset. “During the luteal phase, the reason why your stress threshold is so much lower is because this is the time your body would be incubating a potential baby,” she said. No baby, and you might turn into a frazzled, nagging shrew. Continue reading...
items.content: People have found language for moodiest part of their menstrual cycles, and the right has seized on itEmily Rae Detrick, a health coach, wants women to be more in touch with their menstrual cycle in its entirety. This, she said on an October episode of Culture Apothecary with Alex Clark, a podcast popular among “Make America Healthy Again” (Maha) adherents, is “a way that you honor God and the way we were created”. It also serves her self-assigned mission to help “make America fertile again”.In the episode and on social media, Detrick speaks at length about the luteal phase, the 14-ish days between ovulation and menstruation when women are more likely to experience irritability, bloating and food cravings. If a woman does not get pregnant after ovulating, or so Detrick’s line of thinking goes, her body becomes upset. “During the luteal phase, the reason why your stress threshold is so much lower is because this is the time your body would be incubating a potential baby,” she said. No baby, and you might turn into a frazzled, nagging shrew. Continue reading...

clusters.title: Target ultra-processed food to reduce obesity in Britain | Letter
clusters.neutral_headline: Target ultra-processed food to reduce obesity in Britain | Letter

items.title: Target ultra-processed food to reduce obesity in Britain | Letter
items.standfirst: James Capon responds to an editorial on the health select committee’s call for radical action to regulate food marketsThe MPs’ report mentioned in your editorial is welcome and overdue (The Guardian view on obesity: put public health before food industry pressure, 15 July). The health select committee is right to clarify that obesity is an engineered outcome, not a failure of willpower. But there’s a flaw running through decades of British food policy that risks being repeated: the “high in fat, salt or sugar” framework itself.“High in fat, salt or sugar” combines three nutrients as though they carry equivalent blame. However, the evidence on weight gain and type 2 diabetes points to sugar and refined carbohydrates as the primary drivers, not fat. Salt matters, chiefly when combined with sugar, but the body needs salt to function. Fat matters because without it many fat-soluble vitamins will just pass through our bodies. We need no sugar at all. Continue reading...
items.content: James Capon responds to an editorial on the health select committee’s call for radical action to regulate food marketsThe MPs’ report mentioned in your editorial is welcome and overdue (The Guardian view on obesity: put public health before food industry pressure, 15 July). The health select committee is right to clarify that obesity is an engineered outcome, not a failure of willpower. But there’s a flaw running through decades of British food policy that risks being repeated: the “high in fat, salt or sugar” framework itself.“High in fat, salt or sugar” combines three nutrients as though they carry equivalent blame. However, the evidence on weight gain and type 2 diabetes points to sugar and refined carbohydrates as the primary drivers, not fat. Salt matters, chiefly when combined with sugar, but the body needs salt to function. Fat matters because without it many fat-soluble vitamins will just pass through our bodies. We need no sugar at all. Continue reading...

clusters.title: Fifth person dies in New York City amid legionnaires’ disease outbreak
clusters.neutral_headline: Fifth person dies in New York City amid legionnaires’ disease outbreak
bullet_summary: In an update issued on Tuesday, the department said that 82 cases of legionnaires’ disease have now been identified and five people have died

items.title: Fifth person dies in New York City amid legionnaires’ disease outbreak
items.standfirst: Outbreak has been traced to Upper East Side cooling towers, but Mamdani believes source of exposure was eliminatedA fifth person has died in New York City in connection with the legionnaires’ disease outbreak on Manhattan’s Upper East Side neighborhood, according to the city’s health department.In an update issued on Tuesday, the department said that 82 cases of legionnaires’ disease have now been identified and five people have died. Eight people remain hospitalized, while 56 have been discharged. Continue reading...
items.content: Outbreak has been traced to Upper East Side cooling towers, but Mamdani believes source of exposure was eliminatedA fifth person has died in New York City in connection with the legionnaires’ disease outbreak on Manhattan’s Upper East Side neighborhood, according to the city’s health department.In an update issued on Tuesday, the department said that 82 cases of legionnaires’ disease have now been identified and five people have died. Eight people remain hospitalized, while 56 have been discharged. Continue reading...

clusters.title: CDC cuts leave US less prepared for disease outbreaks, former officials warn
clusters.neutral_headline: CDC cuts leave US less prepared for disease outbreaks, former officials warn

items.title: CDC cuts leave US less prepared for disease outbreaks, former officials warn
items.standfirst: As measles, hantavirus and cyclospora spread, experts say lost staff and expertise are slowing the responseA series of health challenges – hantavirus, screwworm, Ebola, cyclospora, measles, whooping cough, bird flu – are exposing the widening gaps in America’s ability to prevent, identify and halt infectious disease outbreaks.The rapid dismantling of the US public health system at all levels means the US is in worse shape than it was before the Covid pandemic, former top CDC officials say. Continue reading...
